questions.question: Define a Turing Machine and explain its components.
keyPoints: Turing Machine is an abstract computational model invented by Alan Turing in 1936
Components: Infinite tape (memory), Read-Write head, State register, Transition function, Start state, Accept state(s), Reject state(s)
The machine operates based on transition function δ: Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L,R}

questions.question: Distinguish between Turing-decidable and Turing-recognizable languages.
keyPoints: Turing-decidable (recursive): TM always halts on all inputs - gives yes/no answer
Turing-recognizable (recursively enumerable): TM halts and accepts for strings in language, may loop forever for non-members
Every decidable language is recognizable, but not vice versa
Example: Empty language problem is decidable, but Halting Problem is only recognizable (not decidable)

questions.question: What is the Church-Turing Thesis? State its significance.
keyPoints: Church-Turing Thesis: Any effectively computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine
Proposed independently by Alonzo Church and Alan Turing
Significance: Provides a formal definition of 'computable' and 'algorithm'
It cannot be proven but is widely accepted as true - defines the limits of computation

questions.question: Explain the concept of a Universal Turing Machine (UTM).
keyPoints: UTM takes two inputs: encoding of any Turing Machine M, and input string w
It simulates the behavior of M on w
This is the theoretical foundation for stored-program computers
Shows that one machine can perform any computational task given proper instructions

questions.question: What is the Halting Problem? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Halting Problem: Given any program and input, determine if it will halt or run forever
Proved undecidable by Alan Turing in 1936 using diagonalization
Important because it proves there are problems that no algorithm can solve
Shows fundamental limits of computation - not all questions have answers

questions.question: Define configuration of a Turing Machine and give its notation.
keyPoints: Configuration is a complete snapshot of TM at any point during computation
Notation: (current state, tape content, head position) or uqv where q is state, u and v are tape content with head at first symbol of v
Initial configuration: (q_start, w) where w is input
Accepting/Rejecting configurations: when TM enters accept/reject states

questions.question: Explain the difference between Deterministic and Non-Deterministic Turing Machines. Are they equivalent in computational power? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: DTM: For each (state, symbol) pair, exactly one transition defined
NTM: For each (state, symbol) pair, zero or more transitions possible
Both can be simulated by each other - Every NTM can be simulated by DTM using breadth-first search of computation tree
Proof: DTM simulates all branches of NTM - if NTM accepts, DTM finds accepting branch
Time complexity may differ (exponential blow-up possible) but language recognition power is same
Both recognize exactly the recursively enumerable languages

questions.question: Explain the Chomsky hierarchy with respect to machines that recognize each class. Also mention which machines are more powerful than others.
keyPoints: Level 0: Recursively Enumerable - Recognized by Turing Machine (most powerful)
Level 1: Context-Sensitive - Recognized by Linear Bounded Automaton (restricted TM)
Level 2: Context-Free - Recognized by Pushdown Automaton (with stack)
Level 3: Regular - Recognized by Finite Automaton (no memory)
Each level is strictly less powerful than the level above
Turing Machine can simulate all others; FA cannot simulate PDA, etc.

questions.question: What do you understand by undecidability? Prove that the Halting Problem is undecidable using reduction.
keyPoints: Undecidable: No algorithm exists that can solve all instances of the problem (TM cannot always give yes/no answer)
Halting Problem Proof: Assume H(P,w) decides if program P halts on input w
Construct new program D that takes program P, runs H on (P,P), and does opposite - if H says halts, D loops; if H says doesn't halt, D halts
Run D on its own description - contradiction: D halts iff it doesn't halt
Therefore H cannot exist - Halting Problem is undecidable

questions.question: Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language L = {0^n 1^n | n ≥ 1}. Show the transition function and explain its working.
keyPoints: Language: Equal number of 0s followed by equal number of 1s
Strategy: Replace 0s with X moving right, match with Y replacing 1s, then return
Transition function: δ(q0, 0) = (q1, X, R), δ(q1, 0) = (q1, 0, R), δ(q1, 1) = (q2, Y, L), etc.
Process: Replace leftmost 0 with X, move to first 1, replace with Y, move back to find next 0, repeat
When all 0s matched with 1s (all replaced by X and Y), accept
Reject if: 1 appears before 0, or unmatched 0s/1s remain
